--- title: 'random fragment generation' permalink: /tools/fragment-generation --- This is an interactive Python script to generate random DNA fragments from the human genome according to different fragmentation strategies. The user is sequentially prompted to enter all of the information needed for fragment generation when the script is run. The intended purpose was to make generating datasets of random fragments a quick and easy process, especially for people unfamiliar with Python. 2021-04-02-fragment-generation.utf8

Example files

Pertinent files provided on GitHub.

These files include:

  • The base_counts.txt files for both hg19 and hg38 genome builds.

  • Example scripts for how to obtain the individual chromosome fasta files for hg19 and hg38.

  • An interactive script (fragment_generation.py) for generating a single random fragment dataset.

  • An interactive script (fragment_generation_iterative.py) for generating iterative random fragment datasets.

    • This is based on setting a new random seed every N sites (e.g. 100,000)
    • This is beneficial for generating huge random fragment libraries.

  • An example bash script for cleaning and trimming the generated fragment files.

Authorship information

The basis of this script was created in 2006 and 2014 by Peter Cherepanov Peter.Cherepanov@crick.ac.uk.
The code was modified into its current form in 2020 by Greg Bedwell gregoryjbedwell@gmail.com.

An example of running the interactive script

The following video shows how to run the script and example responses to the prompts given to the user for fragment generation.

The prompts are as follows:

  1. The genome build being used.

  2. The path to the chromosome fasta files.

  3. The number of random fragments to generate.

  4. The genome fragmentation of interest MB for MseI/BglII digestion, NASB for NheI/AvrII/SpeI/BamHI digestion, and Random for random fragmentation.

    • If Random, the user will be subsequently prompted to enter:
      1. The mean fragment size.
      2. The standard deviation of the mean fragment size.

  5. The maximum allowed distance to the relevant cut site.

    • I prefer to use a large number here to ensure a faithful sampling of the entire genome. The fragment file can be further trimmed in downstream steps.

Example output

The output of the script is a fasta file containing the sequence information for the fragments generated. This script does not generate a fastq file. For alignment programs like STAR this is not an issue for downstream alignment.

An example of the output is seen below:

## >chr18_strand_1_50097937_random_site_number_0
## GAAGGCTGCAGTGTTGCAATGGAATTCTAGTGTGAAGTTACATGCCATTTTCAGGAGTAAGACTAGTCAAAAGTT
## >chr12_strand_1_31047230_random_site_number_1
## TGACTGTTAGAAGGAAAACTAACAAACAGAAAGGAATAGCATCAACATCAGCAAAAAGGACATCCACACCAAAAG
## >chr10_strand_1_101226057_random_site_number_2
## CAGAAATGGGATCACAATGGGCGTATGTATGTTTGTTACAGAAAATTATTTTGGGGTATCTATTTTT
## >chr13_strand_0_101762183_random_site_number_3
## AACCATCGTATCTAAAATTCCCTGCCCCCAAATCCCCACCTTTGATTTATTTACATGACTTCATTTTACTTTACA
## >chr8_strand_1_19288790_random_site_number_4
## GTGAAATACTGAATGTTCTCCCCATAAGATCAAGAACAAGACAAAGGTGTCTGCTTTCACTATTCAATATTGTAC
